section.title: David and Jonathan's Covenant
section.sectionRationale: David flees to Jonathan in terror for his life, and the two friends renew and deepen the covenant already sworn between them. Jonathan, the crown prince, pledges covenant loving-kindness to David as the LORD's anointed even though it will cost him the throne. They devise a secret sign in the field, Saul's murderous rage boils over even against his own son, and the chapter closes with a tearful, formal farewell sealed in the name of the LORD. It is one movement: costly, self-giving covenant love between the rightful heir and the coming king.
section.thesis: Jonathan, though heir to Saul's throne, freely binds himself in covenant loving-kindness to David, the LORD's anointed, choosing loyalty to God's chosen king over his own claim to the crown, so that a deep friendship becomes a solemn covenant witnessed by the LORD Himself and reaching to their descendants forever.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: David's plea and Jonathan's promise; the covenant renewed with the LORD as witness; the sign of the arrows arranged in the field; Saul's murderous rage against his own son; and the sorrowful, covenant-sealing farewell.

units.label: David's Plea and Jonathan's Promise
units.note: there is but a step between me and death

verses.text: Then David fled from Naioth in Ramah. He came to Jonathan and asked, “What have I done? What is my iniquity? How have I sinned against your father, that he wants to take my life?”
“Far from it!” Jonathan replied. “You will not die. Indeed, my father does nothing, great or small, without telling me. So why would he hide this matter from me? This cannot be true!”

But David again vowed, “Your father knows very well that I have found favor in your eyes, and he has said, ‘Jonathan must not know of this, or he will be grieved.’ As surely as the LORD lives and as you yourself live, there is but a step between me and death.”
Then Jonathan said to David, “Whatever you desire, I will do for you.”
So David told him, “Look, tomorrow is the New Moon, and I am supposed to dine with the king. Instead, let me go and hide in the field until the third evening from now.
If your father misses me at all, tell him, ‘David urgently requested my permission to hurry to Bethlehem, his hometown, because there is an annual sacrifice for his whole clan.’
If he says, ‘Good,’ then your servant is safe, but if he is enraged, you will know he has evil intentions.

Therefore show {{chesed|kindness}} to your servant, for you have brought me into a covenant with you before the LORD. If there is iniquity in me, then kill me yourself; why should you bring me to your father?”
“Never!” Jonathan replied. “If I ever found out that my father had evil intentions against you, would I not tell you?”
Then David asked Jonathan, “Who will tell me if your father answers you harshly?”
“Come,” he replied, “let us go out to the field.” So the two of them went out into the field,

units.label: The Covenant Renewed
units.note: treat me with the LORD's loving devotion, forever

verses.text: and Jonathan said, “By the LORD, the God of Israel, I will sound out my father by this time tomorrow or the next day. If he is favorable toward you, will I not send for you and tell you?

But if my father intends to bring evil on you, then may the LORD punish me, and ever so severely, if I do not tell you and send you on your way in safety. May the LORD be with you, just as He has been with my father.
And as long as I live, treat me with the LORD’s {{chesed|loving devotion}}, that I may not die,
and do not ever cut off your {{chesed|loving devotion}} from my household, not even when the LORD cuts off every one of David’s enemies from the face of the earth.”
So Jonathan made a {{berit|covenant}} with the house of David, saying, “May the LORD hold David’s enemies accountable.”
And Jonathan had David reaffirm his vow out of love for him, for Jonathan loved David as he loved himself.

units.label: The Sign Arranged in the Field
units.note: the LORD is witness between you and me forever

verses.text: Then Jonathan said to David, “Tomorrow is the New Moon, and you will be missed, because your seat will be empty.
When you have stayed three days, hurry down to the place you hid on the day this trouble began, and remain beside the stone Ezel.
I will shoot three arrows to the side of it, as if I were aiming at a target.

Then I will send a boy and say, ‘Go, find the arrows!’ Now, if I expressly say to him, ‘Look, the arrows are on this side of you; bring them,’ then come, because as surely as the LORD lives, it is safe for you, and there is no danger.
But if I say to the young man, ‘Look, the arrows are beyond you,’ then you must go, for the LORD has sent you away.
And as for the matter you and I have discussed, the LORD is a witness between you and me forever.”

units.label: Saul's Rage Against His Own Son
units.note: he is determined to kill David

verses.text: So David hid in the field, and when the New Moon had come, the king sat down to eat.
He sat in his usual place by the wall, opposite Jonathan and beside Abner, but David’s place was empty.
Saul said nothing that day because he thought, “Something has happened to David to make him ceremonially unclean, surely he is unclean.”
But on the day after the New Moon, the second day, David’s place was still empty, and Saul asked his son Jonathan, “Why hasn’t the son of Jesse come to the meal either yesterday or today?”
Jonathan answered, “David urgently requested my permission to go to Bethlehem,

saying, ‘Please let me go, because our clan is holding a sacrifice in the city, and my brother has told me to be there. So now, if I have found favor in your eyes, please let me go and see my brothers.’ That is why he has not come to the king’s table.”

Then Saul’s anger burned against Jonathan, and he said to him, “You son of a perverse and rebellious woman! Do I not know that you have chosen the son of Jesse to your own shame and to the shame of the mother who bore you?
For as long as the son of Jesse lives on this earth, neither you nor your kingdom shall be established. Now send for him and bring him to me, for he must surely die!”
“Why must he be put to death?” Jonathan replied. “What has he done?”
Then Saul hurled his spear at Jonathan to kill him. So Jonathan knew that his father was determined to kill David.
Jonathan got up from the table in fierce anger and did not eat any food that second day of the month, for he was grieved by his father’s shameful treatment of David.

units.label: The Signal and the Sorrowful Farewell

verses.text: In the morning Jonathan went out to the field for the appointment with David, and a small boy was with him.
He said to the boy, “Run and find the arrows I shoot.” And as the boy ran, Jonathan shot an arrow beyond him.
When the boy reached the place where Jonathan’s arrow had fallen, Jonathan called to him, “Isn’t the arrow beyond you?”
Then Jonathan cried out, “Hurry! Make haste! Do not delay!” So the boy picked up the arrow and returned to his master.
But the boy did not know anything; only Jonathan and David knew the arrangement.
Then Jonathan gave his equipment to the boy and said, “Go, take it back to the city.”
When the young man had gone, David got up from the south side of the stone, fell facedown, and bowed three times. Then he and Jonathan kissed each other and wept together, though David wept more.

And Jonathan said to David, “Go in {{shalom|peace}}, for the two of us have sworn in the name of the LORD, saying, ‘May the LORD be a witness between you and me, and between your descendants and mine forever.’” Then David got up and departed, and Jonathan went back into the city.

chesed.gloss: Covenant loyalty, loving-kindness, steadfast love, the loyal love owed and given within a covenant relationship. Jonathan asks David for this chesed as his covenant partner, 20:8, then widens it to embrace his whole household forever, 20:14-15; the same word later describes the LORD's own covenant faithfulness to His people.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a solemn, binding agreement sealed with an oath, often before the LORD as witness. In 20:16 Jonathan formally makes a berit with the house of David, a legally and spiritually binding pledge of loyalty that outlasts both their lifetimes and their families.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being. Jonathan's parting words, 'Go in shalom,' are not a casual goodbye but a covenant blessing, releasing David into God's care as the LORD's anointed and confirming the peace of their sworn bond.

basic.context: David is on the run from Saul and comes to Jonathan desperate to know why the king wants him dead. Jonathan cannot believe it, so together they test Saul with a plan: David will hide, and Jonathan will read his father's reaction at the New Moon feast. In that field, their friendship becomes something more, a covenant, with Jonathan asking David to show covenant loving-kindness to his whole family forever, even after David becomes king in Jonathan's place. Saul's rage proves murderous, even turning his spear on his own son. The chapter closes with the arranged sign of the arrows and a grief-filled farewell, sealed 'in the name of the LORD.' The question the chapter presses is: what does loyal, self-giving love look like when it costs you your own future?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, not a manual for secret codes or family conflict. It really happened, and its enduring point is what it shows about covenant loyalty and self-sacrificial love. Jonathan is the crown prince, yet he does not fight David for the throne; he binds himself to him instead, because he recognizes David as the one the LORD has chosen. That is the timeless lesson beneath the specific plan of arrows and hiding places.
basic.originalLanguages: Jonathan asks David to show him chesed, covenant loving-kindness, and then extends that same chesed to his whole household forever, even beyond his own death. Their bond becomes a formal berit, a covenant, sworn in the name of the LORD Himself as witness. And when they part, Jonathan sends David away with shalom, peace, a covenant blessing rather than a mere goodbye.
basic.typology: Jonathan gives up his own claim to the throne to bind himself to God's anointed king, foreshadowing every believer who lays down self-interest to belong to Christ, the true King. The covenant sworn 'between you and me forever' anticipates the everlasting covenant God makes with His people in Christ, sealed not by an oath alone but by His own blood.
basic.christ: Jonathan, who stood to inherit Saul's throne, instead binds himself in costly, self-giving love to David, the LORD's anointed, asking only that this covenant loving-kindness be kept forever. This looks forward to Christ, the true Anointed One, who Himself gives up everything, His equality with God, to bind Himself to His people in an everlasting covenant, and who calls His followers to lay down their own claims and ambitions to be joined to Him in loyal love.
basic.god: God is the silent third party throughout the chapter, invoked as witness to every promise: 'the LORD is a witness between you and me forever.' He is the one whose covenant loyalty, chesed, both friends appeal to and imitate, and He is sovereign even over Saul's murderous rage, preserving David and the covenant line that will lead to David's throne and ultimately to Christ.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 9 shows David keeping this very covenant, showing chesed to Jonathan's crippled son Mephibosheth for Jonathan's sake. 1 Samuel 18:1-4 records the covenant first made between them. Malachi 2 speaks of the LORD as witness between covenant partners. And Philippians 2 shows Christ emptying Himself for the sake of others, the pattern Jonathan dimly reflects.
basic.oneStory: In the middle of Saul's unraveling kingdom, Jonathan the rightful heir freely binds himself to David, the LORD's anointed but still uncrowned king, in a covenant of costly love witnessed by God Himself. It is one more thread in the long story of God preserving His chosen king against every threat, on the way to the greater Son of David whose covenant love for His people never fails.

context.literary: The chapter is built as a tense sequence of testing, covenant-making, and farewell: David's plea, Jonathan's promise, the secret plan, its execution across the New Moon feast, and the final field meeting. The repeated appeal to the LORD as witness, and the language of chesed and berit, frame what could be read as mere friendship as something legally and spiritually weighty.
context.historical: The New Moon feast was a monthly royal and religious occasion at which the king's officials, including his heir, were expected to be present; an unexplained absence would be noticed immediately. Covenant-making by oath, often sealed with a meal, an exchange, or a solemn declaration before witnesses, was a recognized ancient Near Eastern practice, and invoking God or the gods as witness was standard for binding treaties between parties of unequal status, exactly as Jonathan, the crown prince, does with David.
context.canonical: This chapter deepens the covenant of 1 Samuel 18:1-4 into an explicit, sworn berit that will shape the rest of the David narrative, most visibly when David keeps faith with Mephibosheth in 2 Samuel 9 purely 'for Jonathan's sake.' It sits within the larger movement of Saul's decline and David's rise, showing that even within Saul's own household, God is raising up allies for His anointed king.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jonathan, heir to the throne, willingly subordinating his own claim to David, the LORD's chosen king, in a solemn covenant of loyal love, and to trace how God preserves David through both Jonathan's loyalty and Saul's escalating, murderous jealousy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific covenant, sign, and family conflict in Israel's history; it does not prescribe secret arrow signals or covenant ceremonies for believers today. What endures is the pattern of costly, self-giving loyalty to God's anointed and the danger of jealousy that turns even a father against his own son.

debates.question: Why does Jonathan bind David by covenant rather than compete with him for the throne?

views.label: Faith in God's chosen king
views.case: Jonathan already recognizes, as early as 1 Samuel 18, that David is the one the LORD has chosen; his covenant is an act of faith that submits his own dynastic interest to God's revealed will.

views.label: Covenant friendship deepened by shared danger
views.case: Their bond began as personal friendship, 18:1-4, and this chapter's shared crisis under Saul's threat intensifies it into an explicit, sworn covenant that also secures Jonathan's family's future under the coming king.

debates.bearing: Either way, Jonathan's choice models loyalty to God's purposes over personal ambition, a pattern the New Testament calls every believer to show toward Christ, the true King.

debates.question: How should we read Saul's spear-throw at his own son?

views.label: The climax of Saul's moral collapse
views.case: Saul has already thrown his spear at David twice, 18:11 and 19:10; turning it now on Jonathan shows jealousy and rejection of God's will corrupting even his fatherly love.

views.label: A window into the cost of loyalty to God's anointed
views.case: Jonathan is nearly killed for defending David, showing that standing with God's chosen king can carry real, personal cost, even from those closest to us.

debates.bearing: Both readings warn against the corrosive power of jealousy and commend Jonathan's willingness to suffer for his loyalty to David.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words all cluster around covenant loyalty. Jonathan asks David to show him chesed, 20:8, covenant loving-kindness, the loyal love due within a binding relationship; he then widens the request so that this same chesed will never be cut off from his household, even after David's enemies are destroyed, 20:14-15. Their bond is formalized as a berit, 20:16, a covenant sworn with the LORD invoked as witness, the same kind of solemn, binding agreement Israel used for treaties between unequal parties. And when the two finally part, Jonathan releases David with shalom, 20:42, peace, not a casual farewell but a covenant blessing that entrusts David to God's care and confirms that their sworn bond will hold, 'between you and me forever.'

typology.type: Jonathan surrendering his claim to the throne for David, the LORD's anointed
typology.fulfillment: Every believer laying down self-interest and ambition to belong to Christ, the true and greater King, Philippians 3:7-8.

typology.type: The covenant of chesed sworn 'between you and me forever'
typology.fulfillment: The everlasting covenant God makes with His people in Christ, sealed not merely by an oath but by His own blood, Hebrews 13:20.

typology.type: Jonathan sending David away in shalom under threat of death
typology.fulfillment: Christ's gift of peace to His disciples even as He faced the cross, John 14:27.

study.christ: Jonathan, who by birth stood to inherit Saul's throne, instead binds himself in costly, self-giving love to David, the LORD's anointed, asking only that David's covenant loving-kindness never be cut off from his household. He surrenders his own claim because he trusts God's choice of David more than his own advancement. This dimly foreshadows Christ, the true Anointed King, who though He was in the form of God did not cling to His own place but emptied Himself, and who now binds Himself to His people in an everlasting covenant of chesed, calling every believer, as Jonathan did, to lay down their own claims and ambitions in loyal love to Him.
study.god: God stands behind every promise in this chapter as the invisible, sworn-to witness: 'the LORD is a witness between you and me forever.' He is the source and pattern of the chesed both friends show one another, and His sovereign hand preserves David even as Saul's jealousy turns murderous against his own son. Nothing in Saul's rage can overturn what God has purposed for David's throne, a small but real display of the LORD's unbreakable faithfulness to His covenant promises.

crossReferences.note: The first covenant of love and loyalty between Jonathan and David, which this chapter renews and deepens.
David keeps his covenant chesed to Jonathan's household by caring for Mephibosheth, 'for Jonathan's sake.'
The LORD as witness between covenant partners, the same role invoked between David and Jonathan.
Christ emptying Himself for the sake of others, the pattern of self-giving love Jonathan's covenant foreshadows.
The everlasting covenant sealed in Christ's blood, of which every lesser covenant of loyal love is a shadow.

study.oneStory: In the turmoil of Saul's crumbling reign, Jonathan the rightful heir freely binds himself to David, the LORD's anointed but still uncrowned king, in a covenant of costly, self-giving love sworn before God. Saul's murderous jealousy cannot break what God has purposed, and the covenant sealed here, 'between you and me forever,' becomes one more thread in the long story that runs to David's throne and, at last, to great David's greater Son, whose covenant love for His people never fails.

quiz.q: What does Jonathan ask David to show him and his household, and on what basis?

options.text: The LORD's covenant loving-kindness, chesed, forever, because Jonathan has bound David to himself in a covenant before the LORD.
options.note: Verses 8, 14-15 show Jonathan explicitly asking for chesed rooted in their covenant before the LORD.

options.text: Simple gratitude for a past favor Jonathan once did for David.
options.note: There is real affection and history between them, but the text grounds the request in covenant chesed, not a single repaid favor.

options.text: A guarantee that Jonathan will become David's second-in-command one day.
options.note: That idea appears later in the book, 23:17, but is not what Jonathan asks for here; here he asks for covenant loyalty to his family.

options.text: Nothing at all; Jonathan asks for no promise in return.
options.note: The opposite is true: Jonathan explicitly and repeatedly asks David to promise covenant loving-kindness.

quiz.q: Why is Jonathan's covenant with David so remarkable, given that Jonathan is Saul's heir?

options.text: He willingly binds himself to David, the LORD's anointed, even though it means surrendering his own claim to Saul's throne.
options.note: Verse 17 stresses Jonathan's deep love, and the whole covenant assumes David's household, not Jonathan's, will hold the throne.

options.text: He is simply being a loyal and kind friend to someone he likes.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it understates the self-sacrificial, throne-surrendering scope of what Jonathan does.

options.text: He hopes David will make him second-in-command once David is king.
options.note: Jonathan does say something like this elsewhere, 23:17, but this chapter presents his covenant as selfless loyalty, not a bargain for status.

options.text: He has no real affection for David; the covenant is purely political strategy.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly says Jonathan loved David as he loved himself, the opposite of cold political calculation.

quiz.q: What sign do Jonathan and David arrange so Jonathan can safely warn David of Saul's intentions?

options.text: Jonathan will shoot arrows near the hiding place and tell his servant boy where they landed, a coded message the boy never understands.
options.note: Verses 18-22 and 35-40 detail this arranged sign, kept secret even from the boy who fetches the arrows.

options.text: Jonathan will send a written letter describing Saul's mood.
options.note: No letter is used; the actual method is the spoken arrow signal, chosen precisely because it required no writing.

options.text: Jonathan will simply shout across the field to David directly.
options.note: The whole point of the arrow sign was that Jonathan could not risk speaking to David directly in the open.

options.text: They agree on no sign and David must simply wait and hope.
options.note: A specific, carefully arranged sign is exactly what verses 18-22 describe.

quiz.q: How does Saul respond when Jonathan defends David at the New Moon feast?

options.text: Saul rages that Jonathan's loyalty to David shames the family and threatens Jonathan's own throne, and hurls his spear at his own son.
options.note: Verses 30-33 record Saul's furious words and his spear thrown at Jonathan.

options.text: Saul is disappointed but simply sends Jonathan away from the table.
options.note: Saul does far more than send Jonathan away; he verbally curses him and physically attacks him with a spear.

options.text: Saul quietly agrees that David has done nothing wrong.
options.note: The opposite happens; Saul insists David must die and turns his fury on Jonathan for questioning him.

options.text: Saul praises Jonathan for standing up for what is right.
options.note: Saul curses Jonathan and tries to kill him with a spear; there is no praise in the text.

quiz.q: What does the farewell scene in verse 42 reveal about the nature of this covenant?

options.text: It is sealed 'in the name of the LORD' as witness, extending between their descendants forever, a costly love that points beyond itself to God's own covenant faithfulness.
options.note: Verse 42 explicitly invokes the LORD as witness and extends the covenant to their descendants forever.

options.text: It is a warm but informal goodbye between two close friends.
options.note: Real affection is present, seen in their weeping, but the language of sworn oaths and the LORD as witness marks this as far more than an informal goodbye.

options.text: It mainly confirms that David and Jonathan will never see each other again.
options.note: The text does not state this is a final farewell; its stated purpose is sealing the covenant, not predicting they will never meet.

options.text: It shows the covenant has quietly lapsed under the pressure of Saul's rage.
options.note: The opposite is true: the covenant is explicitly renewed and sealed with an oath at this very moment.

questions: Jonathan risked his father's fury and his own claim to the throne to protect David. Where might God be calling you to costly loyalty, even if it costs you standing, comfort, or advantage?

Jonathan asks David for chesed, covenant loving-kindness, to be shown to his whole household forever. Who has shown you costly, faithful love, and how might you extend that same love to someone who could never repay you?
Saul's jealousy turned him even against his own son. Where might insecurity, envy, or wounded pride be straining your own relationships right now?

The covenant between David and Jonathan was sealed with an oath 'in the name of the LORD' and witnessed by Him. How does knowing that the LORD Himself witnesses your promises shape the commitments you make to others?

Jonathan's self-giving love for David, though he had every reason to compete with him for the throne, points toward Christ's covenant love for His people. How does Christ's unfailing covenant faithfulness to you change how you love people who might outshine or displace you?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew chesed, rendered here 'loving devotion,' covers love, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and covenant loyalty; the BSB footnote notes this range of meaning, so 'covenant loyalty' or 'steadfast love' are equally valid renderings.

The Hebrew text reads simply 'by the wall,' leaving ambiguous whether Jonathan or Abner sat beside Saul; the Septuagint clarifies that Jonathan arose while Abner sat at Saul's side, a reading many translations, including the BSB footnote, follow.

The Hebrew reads 'from the south side' of the stone, while the Septuagint reads 'from beside the stone'; either way David emerges from hiding to bow before Jonathan in a scene of deep grief and covenant love.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 20:1-42. Em dashes present in the source at verses 15, 26, and 41 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in this file.
